does: This paper investigates capability elicitation techniques by introducing password-locked models—neural networks where certain capabilities or behaviors are artificially gated behind learned authentication mechanisms that must be discovered or bypassed. The authors systematically test various elicitation methods against these models to understand when and how techniques succeed or fail at uncovering hidden capabilities. By creating controlled experimental scenarios with explicitly locked behaviors, they can measure the effectiveness of different prompt engineering, fine-tuning, and adversarial approaches in revealing capabilities that models possess but do not normally express.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature examining how to reliably extract or elicit latent capabilities from language models and neural networks more broadly. It extends prior research on prompt engineering, jailbreaking, and adversarial attacks by providing a principled testing framework rather than ad-hoc demonstrations. The paper connects to work on model interpretability and behavior analysis, which seeks to understand what capabilities models truly possess versus what they choose to display based on training. It also engages with research on model safety and alignment, where controlling and understanding capability expression is central to ensuring models behave as intended.
enables: This stress-testing framework enables future work to more rigorously evaluate and compare capability elicitation techniques in a controlled setting before deploying them on real systems. The methodology could support development of more robust defenses against unintended capability extraction and inform better training procedures that prevent undesirable behaviors from being latently present in models. The findings may guide the design of safer fine-tuning methods and highlight failure modes in current alignment techniques, pointing toward better strategies for ensuring models reliably express intended behaviors while resisting unintended activation of problematic capabilities.
